normalized.body:
# 2025-04-15 Therapy

* Muslin
* Aunt threatening to sue my family
* Advisor didn't show up to office hours.
* Thought about what it meant to do something half-assed and came up with the idea that it meant 1. not doing much research beforehand, 2. doing it anyway, 3. realizing it could've been done better afterward. I realized that after I finish pretty much anything the first thing I do is think about how it could have been done better, even if I did a ton of research on it before hand. This is emotionally not terribly healthy, and also highlights that sometimes you have to actually do a thing to learn more about it, you can't just do research and think through all of the possibilities.
* Installed a cross beam to support the hammock poles in the backyard and did it sort of half-assed.
* Ordered a "ChipDrop" and have been moving that today
* Work is overwhelming, too many projects, too many potential conflicts with my values.
* Thought a lot about the mind-body dichotomy, how I live so much in my head and have spent a long time focusing on abstract reasoning, often studying physical labor, or embodied practices, maybe to try to teach myself to care about them more and start to focus on them in my life, but often just keep it abstract.
* Buddhism as abstract philosophy, or thinking through the abstract philosophy and hoping to internalize it so deeply that I act in accordance with the beliefs there, but practice is important. I think I often looked down on people who applied buddhism in practical ways, because it seemed like it was simplifying very complex ideas into "words of wisdom to live by." I was being arrogant and pretentious assuming that anyone who practices in a community has a less pure understanding of the teachings.
* Realized that sometimes you can't expect your mind to spontaneously create the emotional response you want it to in a given context, sometimes you have to intentionally cultivate that emotional response and maybe eventually it will be spontaneous. But it always feels fake or like I'm lying if I express an emotion I don't spontaneously feel.

List order preserved to strictly comply with the no-reorder rule

# 2025-04-15 Therapy

* Muslin
* Aunt threatening to sue my family
* Advisor didn't show up to office hours.
* Thought about what it meant to do something half-assed and came up with the idea that it meant 1. not doing much research beforehand, 2. doing it anyway, 3. realizing it could've been done better afterward. I realized that after I finish pretty much anything the first thing I do is think about how it could have been done better, even if I did a ton of research on it before hand. This is emotionally not terribly healthy, and also highlights that sometimes you have to actually do a thing to learn more about it, you can't just do research and think through all of the possibilities.
* Installed a cross beam to support the hammock poles in the backyard and did it sort of half-assed.
* Ordered a "ChipDrop" and have been moving that today
* Work is overwhelming, too many projects, too many potential conflicts with my values.
* Thought a lot about the mind-body dichotomy, how I live so much in my head and have spent a long time focusing on abstract reasoning, often studying physical labor, or embodied practices, maybe to try to teach myself to care about them more and start to focus on them in my life, but often just keep it abstract.
* Buddhism as abstract philosophy, or thinking through the abstract philosophy and hoping to internalize it so deeply that I act in accordance with the beliefs there, but practice is important. I think I often looked down on people who applied buddhism in practical ways, because it seemed like it was simplifying very complex ideas into "words of wisdom to live by." I was being arrogant and pretentious assuming that anyone who practices in a community has a less pure understanding of the teachings.
* Realized that sometimes you can't expect your mind to spontaneously create the emotional response you want it to in a given context, sometimes you have to intentionally cultivate that emotional response and maybe eventually it will be spontaneous. But it always feels fake or like I'm lying if I express an emotion I don't spontaneously feel.

normalized.summary: This therapy session explored feelings of work-related overwhelm and family stress, alongside reflections on the tension between abstract intellectualization and practical, embodied action. The author recognized a pattern of overthinking and underdoing, acknowledged past arrogance regarding practical Buddhist practice, and discussed the challenge of intentionally cultivating emotions that initially feel forced. Overall, the session highlighted a desire to bridge the gap between mental analysis and lived experience.

normalized.body:
# Of Sleep Disturbances and Academia 9/6/2013

## Sleep Disturbances
The past few nights I have experienced some rather bizarre sleep disturbances. I become half awake and attempt to solve some sort of problem, attempt some sort of task, try to learn some sort of skill. Most nights it has centered on cooking. In a half-conscious state I try to make brittle. I go through the processes in my mind. Boiling down the sugar to make caramel, painting it out on wax paper, letting it cool, trying to cut it into even sections. The night after was city planning. I was not only trying to figure out where things should go, but also working with concrete. Trying to shape it into forms that were pleasing to the eye yet functional. It started to happen again last night but I shut it down. It again was with cooking, a dessert even. I became awake enough to cease the thought though and return to sleep, thankfully. Hopefully these sort of occurrences will cease, they are very tiring.

## Health & Personal Concerns
Kristin has her pap smear this morning. I've had renewed concerns about her being pregnant. I know that the birth control pills should be functioning just fine, still I am uncomfortable about it. I hope she can confirm she isn't today if she gets the pregnancy test there as well. I think she would get an abortion if she were pregnant. I don't think either of us could handle a child right now. I really don't want one myself, not now anyway.

## Academic Work: Paper Draft
I need to catch up on my academic work. I have my paper to write, the first draft of which is due next Wednesday. I have 0 words written for it, but am so far not terribly concerned. It is after all a first draft, though I would like to revise it before handing it in. I would really like to finish half of it at work today. So far work has not proved to be my most productive environment for… work. The lack of windows is stifling. Still it needs to get done and I want it to be good. I want to connect new ideas, form new concepts, utilize new methodologies. Perhaps too much to ask for this early in my academic career, still it would be nice.

## Coursework: Intro to STS
I have also received the first weeks readings for my Intro to STS class. She says the readings are dense and we shouldn't concern ourselves with grasping every idea. She also wants us to print out the articles to bring into class. I hope she isn't the type of professor to require everyone print things out and tries to prevent us from using laptops in class. I learn better with a laptop in front of me. It makes it easier to form connections between the readings and to knowledge I already have. It allows me to search through documents to find the quote that will support my argument. Really it just helps me learn and given that I am paying to be there to learn, I will use what tools best suit my style of learning. The readings don't seem particularly difficult though.

## Class Dynamics & Time Management
My concern is that I will be arrogant in the class. I've already had a year in STS and feel like I will have a better understanding of the material than most of the other students. I need to keep quiet for most of the first class so that I don't end up saying arrogant things. I need to try to figure out a way to either learn something from the class even if it doesn't present any completely new ideas, or get an A in the class without spending too much time on it so that I can pursue other goals such as my super class and my design curriculum. I just hope it doesn't require too much of my time or energy to do well in.

## Current State & Fatigue
I'm feeling very tired. I was hoping the somewhat better sleep I had last night would help me feel more with it today. So far that is not the case. I just want to go back to bed. I also have allergies this morning though, they might be the cause of some of this fatigue. If that's the case I hope the allergies recede so that I can work on my paper with a clear mind.

#journal/creative
#journal/morningpages

## Sleep Disturbances
The past few nights I have experienced some rather bizarre sleep disturbances. I become half awake and attempt to solve some sort of problem, attempt some sort of task, try to learn some sort of skill. Most nights it has centered on cooking. In a half-conscious state I try to make brittle. I go through the processes in my mind. Boiling down the sugar to make caramel, painting it out on wax paper, letting it cool, trying to cut it into even sections. The night after was city planning. I was not only trying to figure out where things should go, but also working with concrete. Trying to shape it into forms that were pleasing to the eye yet functional. It started to happen again last night but I shut it down. It again was with cooking, a dessert even. I became awake enough to cease the thought though and return to sleep, thankfully. Hopefully these sort of occurrences will cease, they are very tiring.

## Health & Personal Concerns

I hope she can confirm she isn't today if she gets the pregnancy test there as well. I think she would get an abortion if she were pregnant. I don't think either of us could handle a child right now. I really don't want one myself, not now anyway.

## Academic Work: Paper Draft

normalized.summary: This excerpt from Jon Peterson's Playing at the World chronicles the evolution of tabletop wargaming into the first role-playing games, focusing on the collaborative efforts of Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson. It details the rise of wargaming clubs like the IFW, the publication of Chainmail, and Arneson's innovative Blackmoor campaign, which introduced key RPG mechanics such as character progression, dungeon exploration, and referee adjudication. Together, these developments laid the foundational systems and cultural context for the creation of Dungeons & Dragons.

normalized.summary: This article introduces Dalit-futurist feminism, a theoretical framework that merges Dalit feminist standpoint theory with cyborg feminism to analyze caste and gender in Indian science fiction. Using Priya Sarukkai Chabria’s Generation 14, the author demonstrates how speculative fiction can mutate and expose the arbitrary structures of Brahmanical patriarchy. The study argues that cyborg figures embody an intersectional, affiliation-based feminist politics that challenges essentialized identities and advocates for inclusive, transformative social alliances.

Lindsay Baker

## Lindsay Baker

> [!profile]
> **VP, Head of Sustainability & Wellbeing**
> **WeWork**
> **Oakland, California**

### Current Role
Head of Sustainability & Wellbeing at WeWork, leading the company's efforts to drive meaningful positive impacts on global and human health.

### Previous Experience
- Industry veteran in sustainable and healthy buildings
- Google's Real Estate team
- Original team at U.S. Green Building Council who built the LEED Rating Systems
- President and Founding Team member of Comfy — Oakland-based smart buildings software company

### Education
- Bachelor's degree in Environmental Studies, Oberlin College
- Master's degree in Building Science, UC Berkeley

### Community Involvement
Active in Bay Area communities and organizations working on building healthier and more sustainable communities:
- Board Member, SPUR (Oakland Board)
- Advisory Board member, Tech Equity Collaborative

normalized.body:
# Data Storage and Retrieval Guidelines

## Core principles (durability & structure)

- Plain-text-first — Prefer Markdown/CSV/TXT over proprietary formats for longevity and diff-ability.
- Open standards — Use formats with stable specs (Markdown, YAML, JSON, CSV, PDF/A, UTF-8).
- Normalization — Enforce consistent encodings and conventions (UTF-8; Unicode NFC; line endings).
- Canonical form — One authoritative representation per item (e.g., .md + YAML) from which others are derived.
- Round-trippability — Any export can be transformed back to the canonical form without loss.
- Separation of concerns — Content lives in files; metadata in YAML; links/indices generated; binaries stored alongside but referenced.
- Idempotent transforms — Re-running pipelines produces the same outputs (no duplicates, stable IDs).
- Deterministic filenames — Use YYYY-MM-DD_slug__ID.md to avoid collisions and ease sorting.

## Identity, metadata, and schema

- Persistent Identifier (PID) — A stable, non-semantic ID (UUID/ULID) that never changes.
- Title vs. ID — Human titles are mutable; PIDs are not. Always key systems on the PID.
- YAML front matter — Lightweight per-note metadata block (id, created, updated, tags, links, type).
- Controlled vocabulary — Curated tag lists to avoid drift (project:whrex, topic:epistemology).
- Taxonomy vs. folksonomy — Structured categories (stable) vs. free tags (flexible); use both.
- Ontology / typed relations — Encode predicates like links:cites|supports|contradicts|derived_from.
- Provenance fields — source, author, method, license, capture_tool to support auditability.
- Timestamps — ISO-8601 UTC (2025-08-27T16:20:00Z) for unambiguous ordering.

## Linking & knowledge structures

- Atomic notes — Small, self-contained ideas that can be recombined (minimize scope creep).
- Zettelkasten links — Intentional, concept-level links that explain “why this connects.”
- Johnny Decimal anchors — Stable numeric neighborhoods for files/folders; IDs appear in YAML.
- Typed links — Relations carry meaning (supports, refutes, examples, uses, needs).
- Backlinks — Automatic reverse references to strengthen navigability.
- Transclusion — Pulling one note’s content into another without duplication.
- Maps of Content (MoC) — Curated “table-of-contents” notes for a domain (index, not a dump).
- Crosswalks — Tables that map your vocab to external schemas (e.g., topic:cooling ↔ schema.org/HeatingSystem).

## Retrieval & ranking (IR/RAG)

- Inverted index — Lexical search over tokens (BM25). Fast and precise for named entities.
- Vector embeddings — Dense representations to capture semantic similarity.
- Hybrid search — Combine lexical (BM25) + vector (ANN) for balanced recall/precision.
- Reranker — Second-stage model that reorders candidates using cross-encoders.
- Faceted search — Filter by metadata facets (date, project, type, tags, people).
- Query expansion — Add synonyms/aliases/lemmas to catch vocabulary variance.
- Chunking — Split long notes into ~300–800 tokens with 10–20% overlap for better recall.
- Deduplication — Hash-based (SHA-256) and fuzzy (simhash/minhash) to reduce noise.
- Evaluation metrics — Precision/Recall/F1, nDCG@k, MRR; keep a small gold set for regression tests.

## Versioning, lineage, and audit

- Git-based versioning — Track diffs for Markdown/YAML; store large binaries via LFS.
- Append-only log — Immutable history of captures/edits; separate from human-facing edits.
- Data lineage — Record how an artifact was derived (inputs → transforms → outputs).
- Fixity — Integrity verification using checksums (store and periodically re-check).
- Audit trail — Who/what changed a record, when, and why (commit messages, changed_by).

## Preservation & backup

- 3-2-1 (or 3-2-1-1-0) — 3 copies, 2 media, 1 off-site; (+1 offline/immutable; 0 restore errors).
- LOCKSS mindset — “Lots Of Copies Keep Stuff Safe” across independent failure domains.
- WORM/immutable storage — Prevents silent corruption/ransomware edits.
- Scrubbing — Scheduled checksum scans to catch bit-rot early.
- Restore testing — Periodic, scripted drills that prove backups actually restore.
- Export matrix — Routine bulk exports (zip of .md+YAML; CSV; JSON-LD; PDF/A) to reduce lock-in risk.

## Privacy, security, and ethics

- Data minimization — Keep only what you need, for clear purposes; reduce breach impact.
- Classification — Label sensitivity (public/internal/private/secret) to drive handling rules.
- RBAC/ABAC — Role/attribute-based access; least privilege by default.
- Encryption — At rest and in transit; keys stored/rotated in a proper keystore.
- Redaction — Remove or mask PII/secrets before broad sharing or model training.
- Consent & purpose limitation — Respect original purpose; obtain consent for secondary use.
- Retention schedule — Time-bound deletion (with explicit exceptions) to reduce risk and honor impermanence.
- Threat model — Enumerate likely risks (loss, leak, tamper) and mitigations (off-site, MFA, immutability).

## Automation & pipelines

- ETL/ELT — Capture → normalize → enrich → index; store raw alongside processed.
- Idempotence — Jobs can rerun without duplicating work or drifting state.
- Checkpointing — Persist progress so long runs resume safely.
- Backfill — Reprocess historical data after schema/model changes.
- Observability — Logs, metrics, and alerts for pipeline health and latency.
- Validation — Schemas (JSON Schema) + unit tests for transforms to keep data clean.

## Quality & curation workflows

- Inbox capture — Low-friction dumping ground with automatic timestamping/IDs.
- Triage — Quick pass: rename, assign type, minimal tags, link to 1–2 neighbors.
- Progressive summarization — Layered summaries (tldr, abstract, key_points, full).
- Review cadence — Weekly “gardening” to merge duplicates, promote MoCs, prune.
- Epistemic status — Self-rated confidence (speculative, working, settled) to guide reuse.

## Interop & export

- Pandoc-friendly Markdown — Avoid tool-specific extensions; keep images local with relative paths.
- YAML cross-refs — Store external IDs (DOI, URL, invoice#, IRB#) for federation.
- JSON-LD sidecars — Optional machine-readable mirrors for future graph tools.
- Data contracts — Define what downstream tools can expect (fields/types/invariants).

---

## Minimal canonical schema (for Markdown notes)

```yaml
---
id: ulid_01JABCDXYZ7H1X3M5R9P8
title: Short human-readable title
type: journal|project|research|reference|moc|task|source
created: 2025-08-27T09:00:00Z
updated: 2025-08-27T09:00:00Z
jd_code: 12.34  # if using Johnny Decimal
tags: [topic:epistemology, project:dissertation, person:gillian]
sensitivity: private
status: working  # speculative|working|settled
provenance:
  source: "obsidian"
  capture_tool: "mobile-quicknote"
  author: "Ellie"
links:
  cites: [ulid_..., ulid_...]
  supports: []
  contradicts: []
  derived_from: []
summary:
  tldr: >
    One-sentence essence for quick recall.
  key_points:
    - Point 1
    - Point 2
---
# H1 Main content
Free-form Markdown content with intentional links to [[other-notes-by-ID-or-slug]] and lightweight headings.
```

## File naming convention (deterministic)

- YYYY-MM-DD__slug__ULID.md (e.g., 2025-08-27__hydrocele-notes__01JABCDXYZ7H...md).
- Binaries: assets/01JABCDXYZ7H.../image_001.jpg referenced relatively from the note.

---

## Heuristics your AI should follow (actionable rules)

- Create first, label immediately — On capture, assign ULID, created, type, 1–3 tags, and a draft title.
- Link with intent — When adding a link, choose a type (cites|supports|contradicts|example_of|used_in) and write a 1-line rationale.
- Prefer IDs over titles — Maintain links by PID; update human titles freely.
- Chunk for retrieval — For indexing/RAG, split content into 400–600-token chunks with 80-token overlap; store chunk IDs as id#chunk_n.
- Hybrid retrieval default — Search = BM25 top-200 → rerank with cross-encoder → vector fallback if low lexical recall.
- Summarize on touch — Any edit triggers (a) update updated, (b) refresh tldr and key_points.
- Deduplicate — Before saving, check simhash/minhash against recent items; if ≥90% similar, link as duplicate_of rather than create a new node.
- Respect sensitivity — Don’t surface private|secret items in broad suggestions unless the query is clearly personal and local.
- Export routinely — Nightly job to produce .zip of canonical .md+YAML, plus CSV and JSON-LD indexes.
- Test restores — Weekly restore a random sample (yes, actually open them).
- Retention with compassion — Apply deletion/archival rules, but ask for consent on edge cases involving other people’s privacy.
- Epistemic humility — Attach status and confidence to generated summaries; avoid overstating certainty.
- Small kindnesses — Prefer clarity over cleverness; if a link is ambiguous, suggest disambiguation rather than guessing.

## Author Note

If you want, I can turn this into a machine-readable “policy file” (YAML/JSON) your agent can load at startup and validate against. Also happy to tailor the schema to your current JDS ranges and Obsidian conventions.
warnings: Converted bullet characters (•) to standard Markdown list markers (-) for improved Obsidian rendering.
Added '## Author Note' heading to structurally separate the closing conversational paragraph.

parsed.body:
# Data Storage and Retrieval Guidelines

## Core principles (durability & structure)

- Plain-text-first — Prefer Markdown/CSV/TXT over proprietary formats for longevity and diff-ability.
- Open standards — Use formats with stable specs (Markdown, YAML, JSON, CSV, PDF/A, UTF-8).
- Normalization — Enforce consistent encodings and conventions (UTF-8; Unicode NFC; line endings).
- Canonical form — One authoritative representation per item (e.g., .md + YAML) from which others are derived.
- Round-trippability — Any export can be transformed back to the canonical form without loss.
- Separation of concerns — Content lives in files; metadata in YAML; links/indices generated; binaries stored alongside but referenced.
- Idempotent transforms — Re-running pipelines produces the same outputs (no duplicates, stable IDs).
- Deterministic filenames — Use YYYY-MM-DD_slug__ID.md to avoid collisions and ease sorting.

## Identity, metadata, and schema

- Persistent Identifier (PID) — A stable, non-semantic ID (UUID/ULID) that never changes.
- Title vs. ID — Human titles are mutable; PIDs are not. Always key systems on the PID.
- YAML front matter — Lightweight per-note metadata block (id, created, updated, tags, links, type).
- Controlled vocabulary — Curated tag lists to avoid drift (project:whrex, topic:epistemology).
- Taxonomy vs. folksonomy — Structured categories (stable) vs. free tags (flexible); use both.
- Ontology / typed relations — Encode predicates like links:cites|supports|contradicts|derived_from.
- Provenance fields — source, author, method, license, capture_tool to support auditability.
- Timestamps — ISO-8601 UTC (2025-08-27T16:20:00Z) for unambiguous ordering.

## Linking & knowledge structures

- Atomic notes — Small, self-contained ideas that can be recombined (minimize scope creep).
- Zettelkasten links — Intentional, concept-level links that explain “why this connects.”
- Johnny Decimal anchors — Stable numeric neighborhoods for files/folders; IDs appear in YAML.
- Typed links — Relations carry meaning (supports, refutes, examples, uses, needs).
- Backlinks — Automatic reverse references to strengthen navigability.
- Transclusion — Pulling one note’s content into another without duplication.
- Maps of Content (MoC) — Curated “table-of-contents” notes for a domain (index, not a dump).
- Crosswalks — Tables that map your vocab to external schemas (e.g., topic:cooling ↔ schema.org/HeatingSystem).

## Retrieval & ranking (IR/RAG)

- Inverted index — Lexical search over tokens (BM25). Fast and precise for named entities.
- Vector embeddings — Dense representations to capture semantic similarity.
- Hybrid search — Combine lexical (BM25) + vector (ANN) for balanced recall/precision.
- Reranker — Second-stage model that reorders candidates using cross-encoders.
- Faceted search — Filter by metadata facets (date, project, type, tags, people).
- Query expansion — Add synonyms/aliases/lemmas to catch vocabulary variance.
- Chunking — Split long notes into ~300–800 tokens with 10–20% overlap for better recall.
- Deduplication — Hash-based (SHA-256) and fuzzy (simhash/minhash) to reduce noise.
- Evaluation metrics — Precision/Recall/F1, nDCG@k, MRR; keep a small gold set for regression tests.

## Versioning, lineage, and audit

- Git-based versioning — Track diffs for Markdown/YAML; store large binaries via LFS.
- Append-only log — Immutable history of captures/edits; separate from human-facing edits.
- Data lineage — Record how an artifact was derived (inputs → transforms → outputs).
- Fixity — Integrity verification using checksums (store and periodically re-check).
- Audit trail — Who/what changed a record, when, and why (commit messages, changed_by).

## Preservation & backup

- 3-2-1 (or 3-2-1-1-0) — 3 copies, 2 media, 1 off-site; (+1 offline/immutable; 0 restore errors).
- LOCKSS mindset — “Lots Of Copies Keep Stuff Safe” across independent failure domains.
- WORM/immutable storage — Prevents silent corruption/ransomware edits.
- Scrubbing — Scheduled checksum scans to catch bit-rot early.
- Restore testing — Periodic, scripted drills that prove backups actually restore.
- Export matrix — Routine bulk exports (zip of .md+YAML; CSV; JSON-LD; PDF/A) to reduce lock-in risk.

## Privacy, security, and ethics

- Data minimization — Keep only what you need, for clear purposes; reduce breach impact.
- Classification — Label sensitivity (public/internal/private/secret) to drive handling rules.
- RBAC/ABAC — Role/attribute-based access; least privilege by default.
- Encryption — At rest and in transit; keys stored/rotated in a proper keystore.
- Redaction — Remove or mask PII/secrets before broad sharing or model training.
- Consent & purpose limitation — Respect original purpose; obtain consent for secondary use.
- Retention schedule — Time-bound deletion (with explicit exceptions) to reduce risk and honor impermanence.
- Threat model — Enumerate likely risks (loss, leak, tamper) and mitigations (off-site, MFA, immutability).

## Automation & pipelines

- ETL/ELT — Capture → normalize → enrich → index; store raw alongside processed.
- Idempotence — Jobs can rerun without duplicating work or drifting state.
- Checkpointing — Persist progress so long runs resume safely.
- Backfill — Reprocess historical data after schema/model changes.
- Observability — Logs, metrics, and alerts for pipeline health and latency.
- Validation — Schemas (JSON Schema) + unit tests for transforms to keep data clean.

## Quality & curation workflows

- Inbox capture — Low-friction dumping ground with automatic timestamping/IDs.
- Triage — Quick pass: rename, assign type, minimal tags, link to 1–2 neighbors.
- Progressive summarization — Layered summaries (tldr, abstract, key_points, full).
- Review cadence — Weekly “gardening” to merge duplicates, promote MoCs, prune.
- Epistemic status — Self-rated confidence (speculative, working, settled) to guide reuse.

## Interop & export

- Pandoc-friendly Markdown — Avoid tool-specific extensions; keep images local with relative paths.
- YAML cross-refs — Store external IDs (DOI, URL, invoice#, IRB#) for federation.
- JSON-LD sidecars — Optional machine-readable mirrors for future graph tools.
- Data contracts — Define what downstream tools can expect (fields/types/invariants).

---

## Minimal canonical schema (for Markdown notes)

```yaml

title: Short human-readable title
type: journal|project|research|reference|moc|task|source
created: 2025-08-27T09:00:00Z

jd_code: 12.34  # if using Johnny Decimal
```

- Epistemic humility — Attach status and confidence to generated summaries; avoid overstating certainty.
- Small kindnesses — Prefer clarity over cleverness; if a link is ambiguous, suggest disambiguation rather than guessing.

normalized.summary: This Summary of Care documents the evaluation and scheduled surgical management for Ellian Eorwyn's rapidly growing right hydrocele and mild left varicocele. A scrotal ultrasound confirmed the hydrocele and epididymal cysts while ruling out testicular torsion or masses. The patient is scheduled for a right hydrocelectomy and varicocelectomy, with active management of comorbidities including depression, anxiety, and a history of inguinal hernia repairs.

# Living Well

## Body

- [x] Don't Smoke
- [x] Exercise (walking)
- [x] Fiber, plant foward diet
- [x] Sleep 7+ hours per night
- [ ] Meditate
- [ ] Read
- [ ] Write
- [ ] Exercise (strength)
- [ ] Keep Alcohol low
- [ ] Reduce Isolation

## Environment

- [x] Don't Drive
- [x] Don't eat beef or lamb
- [x] Create habitat
- [ ] Electrify
- [ ] Buy fewer new things

## Giving

- [ ] 30% [GiveWell Top Charities Fund](https://www.givewell.org/top-charities-fund)
- [x] 20% [Partners In Health](https://www.pih.org/donate)
- [ ] 20% [Founders Pledge Climate Fund](https://www.founderspledge.com/funds/climate-fund/about)
- [ ] 10% [GiveDirectly](https://www.givedirectly.org/)
- [ ] 10% [Teaching at the Right Level Africa](https://teachingattherightlevel.org/donate/)
- [x] 10% [Complexly](https://secure.givelively.org/donate/complexly-inc)
warnings: Original contains typo 'plant foward diet'; preserved verbatim per constraints.
